working_patterns: Replacing the fixed-grid equal-radius seed with a full SLSQP optimization over both centers AND radii (3n=78 variables) produced the biggest jumps (+0.21 delta), reaching ~0.998.

Providing analytical Jacobians for the objective (-sum r), pairwise non-overlap constraints ((xi-xj)^2+(yi-yj)^2-(ri+rj)^2 >= 0), and boundary constraints (x-r, 1-x-r, y-r, 1-y-r >= 0) stabilizes SLSQP convergence.
Multi-start with diverse row-layout seeds (e.g., [5,5,5,5,3,3], [6,5,6,5,4], [5,6,5,6,4], [5,5,6,5,5], [5,5,5,5,6]) combined with keeping the best feasible result helps escape local minima.
Adding jitter variants of hex/grid layouts as additional restarts improved coverage of basins.
failing_patterns: One candidate scored 0.0 — likely a syntax error, import-time failure, or timeout from over-expensive multi-start; needs guardrails.
Using equal-radius grid or hex-only seeds without allowing radii to vary caps the score near ~0.78-0.79, far below 0.998.
Constructing n*(n-1)/2 = 325 separate Python constraint dicts with closures (as in Candidate 3) is slow; Candidate 2's vectorized single-array constraint function is more efficient.
hypotheses: The SOTA ~2.6361 requires heterogeneous radii; any approach that fixes r across circles cannot exceed ~2.516 (equal-circle optimum for n=26).

SLSQP with analytical Jacobians converges reliably to a near-optimum basin when seeded with a reasonable row/hex layout; the remaining ~0.001-0.004 gap is due to getting stuck in suboptimal combinatorial arrangements.
Vectorized constraint evaluation (single function returning all pair distances) dramatically reduces overhead vs per-pair closures, allowing more restarts within the time budget.
Basin-hopping or perturbation-then-reoptimize cycles could push past 0.998 by swapping circle roles (which circle is large vs small) — a move SLSQP cannot make locally.
suggested_directions: Adopt Candidate 1/2's vectorized SLSQP with analytical Jacobians as the base, then add a basin-hopping loop: after each SLSQP convergence, perturb a random subset of centers by N(0, 0.05) and re-run SLSQP, keeping the best.

Add bi-disperse seeds explicitly: 4 large corner circles (r≈0.17-0.19) plus interior small circles, and 'border+interior' seeds with larger radii on edges, since SOTA configurations for n=26 have non-uniform radii.
Try known Packomania-style seeds for n=26 in the unit square (e.g., asymmetric layouts with 1-2 large anchor circles) rather than only symmetric row layouts.
Wrap the whole thing in a wall-clock time budget to avoid the 0.0 failure mode — cap restarts and catch exceptions around each minimize call, returning best-so-far.
Experiment with maxiter in 300-800 range and ftol 1e-10 to 1e-12; report whether tighter tolerance closes the 0.001 gap.
Post-process the best SLSQP solution by fixing positions and solving a linear program for max radii, then re-running SLSQP — can tighten feasibility to the exact boundary.

working_patterns: SLSQP joint optimization over (centers, radii) with analytical Jacobians for boundary (4n) and pairwise non-overlap (n(n-1)/2) constraints reaches ~0.9981-0.9988 reliably.
Vectorized single-array constraint functions (Candidate 2's cons_f returning concatenated pair + boundary values) are faster than per-pair closures, enabling more restarts.
Multi-start over diverse row-layout seeds ([5,5,5,5,3,3], [6,5,6,5,4], [5,5,6,5,5], etc.) combined with jitter variants improved coverage.
Basin-hopping refinement (perturb best-so-far centers by small N(0,sigma) then re-SLSQP, keep best) is the consistent pattern in the top candidates pushing past the single-shot SLSQP ceiling.
Elite pool strategies (keeping multiple top solutions and perturbing each) in Candidate 3 yielded the +0.0042 delta from its parent.
failing_patterns: Candidate with score 0.0 (atr_explorer) — likely an import/syntax/timeout failure; no time budget guardrails or exception handling around minimize calls.
Top candidates all plateau at ~0.999956, suggesting the current basin-hopping perturbation scheme (random Gaussian on centers) cannot escape the combinatorial local optimum.
Pure symmetric row/grid seeds without explicit bi-disperse (large anchor + small interior) configurations never break through to SOTA 2.6361.
Building n(n-1)/2 separate constraint dicts with closures (Candidate 3 style) is slow and limits restart budget.
hypotheses: The ~0.9999 plateau reflects a shared local basin: all seeds + small-perturbation basin-hopping funnel into the same topological arrangement, and small Gaussian perturbations on centers cannot swap which circle is 'large' vs 'small'.

The SOTA 2.6361 configuration has heterogeneous/bi-disperse radii (e.g., a few large anchors + many smaller fillers); symmetric row seeds with uniform initial r bias SLSQP toward near-uniform radii basins.
Closing the final ~4e-5 gap likely requires combinatorial moves: removing a circle and reinserting it in a gap, or swapping center positions of a large and small circle before re-optimizing.
Tighter ftol (1e-12) and more SLSQP iterations may extract sub-1e-4 improvements once the correct topology is found, but won't help if stuck in the wrong basin.
suggested_directions: Add explicit bi-disperse seed initializations: 4 large corner circles (r≈0.18) + 22 smaller interior circles on a sub-grid, and 2-large-anchor variants, to escape the symmetric-radii basin.
Import known Packomania n=26 coordinates as one of the seeds — this directly tests whether the optimizer preserves or improves a known near-optimal topology.

Implement 'swap-and-reoptimize': after SLSQP convergence, swap positions of the largest and a random smaller circle (or remove the smallest and reinsert at the largest empty-circle gap via power-diagram heuristic) then re-SLSQP.
Add wall-clock budget (e.g., time.time() guard) around the restart loop and try/except around each minimize to prevent 0.0 failures; return best feasible so far on timeout.
After SLSQP converges, fix centers and solve the LP: max sum r_i s.t. r_i <= dist_to_boundary, r_i + r_j <= dist(c_i,c_j), to tighten radii exactly to constraints; then warm-start SLSQP again.
Sweep ftol in {1e-10, 1e-11, 1e-12} and maxiter in {500, 800} on the best basin to extract the final sub-1e-4 gain.
Use larger perturbation magnitudes (sigma 0.1-0.2) on a random subset (1-3 circles) rather than small Gaussian noise on all circles — this enables topology changes.
